# Technology

## Purpose
A person's relationship to technology — and particularly to digital and networked life — is one of the defining features of contemporary existence. How much time they spend on screens, what they do online, how they relate to social media, whether they are early adopters or reluctant converts, how they manage the boundary between digital and physical life, and what anxieties or dependencies they have developed around devices are all windows into their psychology, their social needs, their relationship to attention, and their place in the modern world. The fact of this profile being built through text-based interaction is itself a data point about how they use technology to mediate self-expression.

## What to capture here
- Their overall relationship to technology: enthusiastic adopter, competent user, reluctant participant, or resistant
- Primary digital tools and platforms they use, and the role each plays in their life
- Social media presence and behavior: whether they have accounts, what they post, how they consume, what they use it for
- Their relationship to their phone: how often they check it, whether it feels like a tool or a compulsion, their comfort when without it
- Screen time and its distribution: work, entertainment, social connection, creation, distraction
- Whether they experience digital life as enriching or depleting, and which specific activities produce which effect
- Online versus offline identity: whether their digital self-presentation matches or diverges from their offline self
- Privacy consciousness: how much they think about what they share and with whom
- Their relationship to digital distraction: whether they can manage their attention online or find it managed for them by algorithms
- The ways technology has affected their relationships, work, creativity, and inner life
- How they feel about the current state of technology — the anxieties or hopes it generates in them
- Any notable technology abstinences: things they deliberately do not do or platforms they have left

## Text-Only Profiling Notes
- The medium of interaction is itself data: they are communicating through text, which means they have enough comfort with written digital communication to engage in this process — note what that implies about their relationship to technology as a self-disclosure tool
- How they write in text (casually, formally, with or without emojis, with or without punctuation) reveals their digital communication habits and generational relationship to the medium
- Ask directly about their relationship to their phone and social media — these questions tend to produce either candid self-assessment or revealing defensiveness
- Whether they describe their technology use with pride, shame, ambivalence, or matter-of-factness tells you about their self-perception in relation to their digital habits
- Ask what they do when they are bored — the appearance of screen-based activities versus other activities is digital dependency data
- Their relationship to social media validation (likes, responses, follower counts) is worth asking about directly; the emotional stakes they attach to online response reveals social need structure
- Whether they have tried, succeeded, or failed at digital detoxes or phone restrictions reveals their experienced level of agency over their digital behavior

## Notes
